text: What mass of CaCOâ‚ƒ is required to produce 5.6 L of COâ‚‚ at STP on complete decomposition? (CaCOâ‚ƒ â†’ CaO + COâ‚‚; molar mass CaCOâ‚ƒ = 100 g molâپ»آ¹)
explanation: 5.6 L at STP = 5.6/22.4 = 0.25 mol COâ‚‚. Stoichiometry is 1:1, so 0.25 mol CaCOâ‚ƒ is needed = 0.25 أ— 100 = 25 g. Option (2).

text: An organic compound contains 40% carbon, 6.67% hydrogen and the rest oxygen by mass. Its empirical formula is:
The total number of unpaired electrons in the ground-state configuration of a Cr atom (Z = 24) is:
explanation: Cr has the anomalous configuration [Ar]3dâپµ4sآ¹ (extra stability of the half-filled 3d and half-filled 4s). All five 3d electrons are unpaired and the single 4s electron is unpaired, giving a total of 5 + 1 = 6 unpaired electrons. Option (4).

text: The energy of an electron in the ground state of a hydrogen atom is âˆ’13.6 eV. The energy required to excite the electron from n = 1 to n = 2 is:
explanation: Eâ‚پ = âˆ’13.6 eV; Eâ‚‚ = âˆ’13.6/2آ² = âˆ’3.4 eV. خ”E = Eâ‚‚ âˆ’ Eâ‚پ = âˆ’3.4 âˆ’ (âˆ’13.6) = 10.2 eV. Option (2).

text: For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal reversible expansion, which of the following is true?
explanation: For an ideal gas, U depends only on temperature; isothermal means خ”T = 0 so خ”U = 0 (and خ”H = 0). By first law خ”U = q + w = 0, hence q = âˆ’w. During expansion the gas does work (w negative) and absorbs heat (q positive). Option (1).

text: Given C(s) + Oâ‚‚(g) â†’ COâ‚‚(g), خ”H = âˆ’393.5 kJ molâپ»آ¹ and CO(g) + آ½Oâ‚‚(g) â†’ COâ‚‚(g), خ”H = âˆ’283.0 kJ molâپ»آ¹. The enthalpy of formation of CO(g) is:
explanation: C + آ½Oâ‚‚ â†’ CO. By Hess's law subtract the second equation from the first: خ”H = (âˆ’393.5) âˆ’ (âˆ’283.0) = âˆ’110.5 kJ molâپ»آ¹. Option (1).

text: The pH of a 0.001 M HCl solution at 25 آ°C is:
explanation: HCl is a strong acid, fully dissociated: [Hâپ؛] = 0.001 = 10âپ»آ³ M. pH = âˆ’log(10âپ»آ³) = 3. Option (2).

text: The solubility product (Ksp) of AgCl is 1.8 أ— 10âپ»آ¹âپ° at 25 آ°C. Its molar solubility in pure water is approximately:
explanation: For AgCl â‡Œ Agâپ؛ + Clâپ», Ksp = sآ². s = âˆڑ(1.8 أ— 10âپ»آ¹âپ°) = 1.34 أ— 10âپ»âپµ M. Option (2).

text: Standard electrode potentials are Eآ°(Znآ²âپ؛/Zn) = âˆ’0.76 V and Eآ°(Cuآ²âپ؛/Cu) = +0.34 V. The standard EMF of the Daniell cell (Zn | Znآ²âپ؛ || Cuآ²âپ؛ | Cu) is:
explanation: Eآ°cell = Eآ°cathode âˆ’ Eآ°anode = Eآ°(Cuآ²âپ؛/Cu) âˆ’ Eآ°(Znآ²âپ؛/Zn) = 0.34 âˆ’ (âˆ’0.76) = +1.10 V. Option (2).

text: A current of 1.93 A is passed through molten AlClâ‚ƒ for 1 hour. The mass of aluminium (atomic mass 27, F = 96500 C) deposited at the cathode is approximately:
explanation: Charge Q = I أ— t = 1.93 أ— 3600 = 6948 C. Alآ³âپ؛ + 3eâپ» â†’ Al, so equivalent mass = 27/3 = 9 g per 96500 C. Mass = (9 أ— 6948)/96500 = 62532/96500 â‰ˆ 0.648 g â‰ˆ 0.65 g. Option (2).

text: For a first-order reaction, the half-life is 20 minutes. The time required for the reactant concentration to fall to 1/8 of its initial value is:
explanation: For first order, half-life is constant. 1/8 = (1/2)آ³, i.e. three half-lives. Time = 3 أ— 20 = 60 min. Option (2).

text: The rate constant of a reaction roughly doubles for every 10 آ°C rise in temperature. This empirical observation is best explained by:
options: A decrease in activation energy with temperature
An increase in the fraction of molecules with energy â‰¥ Ea (Arrhenius/Maxwellâ€“Boltzmann)
A decrease in the number of collisions
An increase in the threshold energy
explanation: As temperature rises, the Maxwellâ€“Boltzmann distribution shifts so a much larger fraction of molecules has energy â‰¥ Ea; via the Arrhenius equation k = A e^(âˆ’Ea/RT), k increases sharply (â‰ˆ doubles per 10 آ°C). Ea itself is essentially constant. Option (2).

text: The molality of a solution prepared by dissolving 9.8 g of Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ (molar mass 98 g molâپ»آ¹) in 500 g of water is:
explanation: Moles Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ = 9.8/98 = 0.1 mol. Molality = moles solute / kg solvent = 0.1/0.5 = 0.2 m. Option (2).

text: An aqueous solution of glucose (non-volatile, non-electrolyte) boils at a higher temperature than pure water. The colligative property responsible and its key feature is:
options: Elevation of boiling point, depends on number of solute particles
Osmotic pressure, depends on solute identity
Lowering of vapour pressure that depends on solute mass
explanation: A non-volatile solute lowers the vapour pressure, raising the boiling point. This boiling-point elevation (خ”Tb = iآ·Kbآ·m) is a colligative property depending on the number of solute particles, not their identity. Option (1).

text: In the reaction MnOâ‚„âپ» + 8Hâپ؛ + 5Feآ²âپ؛ â†’ Mnآ²âپ؛ + 5Feآ³âپ؛ + 4Hâ‚‚O, the change in oxidation number of manganese is:
explanation: In MnOâ‚„âپ», Mn is +7; in Mnآ²âپ؛ it is +2. Mn is reduced, gaining 5 electrons (each Feآ²âپ؛â†’Feآ³âپ؛ supplies 1 eâپ», and 5 Feآ²âپ؛ supply 5 eâپ»). Option (1).

text: The equilibrium constant Kc for a reaction is 4 أ— 10âپ»آ². At equilibrium the reaction mixture, compared to standard state, indicates that:
options: The reaction has gone to completion
explanation: Kc = 0.04 < 1, so at equilibrium the reactant concentrations exceed product concentrations; the position of equilibrium lies toward the reactants. A small K corresponds to a positive خ”Gآ°. Option (2).

text: Which of the following ions has the smallest ionic radius?
explanation: Naâپ؛, Mgآ²âپ؛, Alآ³âپ؛ and Fâپ» are isoelectronic (10 electrons each). For isoelectronic species, ionic radius decreases as nuclear charge increases. Alآ³âپ؛ (Z = 13) has the highest nuclear charge, hence the smallest radius. Option (3).

text: Among the following, the element with the highest first ionization enthalpy is:
explanation: Nitrogen (2pآ³, half-filled stable) has an anomalously high first ionization enthalpy, greater than oxygen (2pâپ´), whose paired electron experiences extra repulsion making one electron easier to remove. So order is N > O > C > B. Option (3).

text: According to VSEPR theory, the shape of the SFâ‚„ molecule (S has one lone pair) is:
explanation: SFâ‚„ has 4 bond pairs + 1 lone pair = 5 electron domains (spآ³d, trigonal bipyramidal arrangement). The lone pair occupies an equatorial position, giving a see-saw shape. Option (3).

text: The correct order of bond order for Oâ‚‚, Oâ‚‚âپ؛ and Oâ‚‚âپ» according to molecular orbital theory is:
explanation: Bond order = (Nb âˆ’ Na)/2. Oâ‚‚ has bond order 2.0. Removing an electron from an antibonding د€* orbital (Oâ‚‚âپ؛) raises it to 2.5; adding an electron to د€* (Oâ‚‚âپ») lowers it to 1.5. Hence Oâ‚‚âپ؛ (2.5) > Oâ‚‚ (2.0) > Oâ‚‚âپ» (1.5). Option (1).

text: In the coordination compound [Co(NHâ‚ƒ)â‚†]Clâ‚ƒ, the coordination number and oxidation state of cobalt are respectively:
explanation: Six NHâ‚ƒ ligands (each neutral, monodentate) give coordination number 6. The complex ion charge is +3 (balanced by 3 Clâپ»); since NHâ‚ƒ is neutral, Co is +3. Option (2).

text: Which of the following complexes will exhibit geometrical (cisâ€“trans) isomerism?
explanation: Square-planar MAâ‚‚Bâ‚‚ complexes such as [Pt(NHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚Clâ‚‚] show cisâ€“trans isomerism. [Co(NHâ‚ƒ)â‚†]آ³âپ؛ has identical ligands (no geometrical isomers), [Ni(CN)â‚„]آ²âپ» has four identical ligands, and [Co(en)â‚ƒ]آ³âپ؛ shows optical (not geometrical) isomerism. Option (2).

text: Among the hydrides of group 15 elements (NHâ‚ƒ, PHâ‚ƒ, AsHâ‚ƒ, SbHâ‚ƒ), the one with the highest boiling point is:
explanation: NHâ‚ƒ is anomalously high due to hydrogen bonding, but among PHâ‚ƒ, AsHâ‚ƒ, SbHâ‚ƒ boiling point rises with molecular size. The overall highest boiling point belongs to SbHâ‚ƒ (largest, strongest van der Waals forces), which exceeds even NHâ‚ƒ. Order: SbHâ‚ƒ > NHâ‚ƒ > AsHâ‚ƒ > PHâ‚ƒ. Option (4).

text: The brown ring test for nitrate ions gives a brown complex whose formula is:
explanation: In the brown ring test, NO produced by reduction of nitrate combines with Feآ²âپ؛ to form the brown complex [Fe(Hâ‚‚O)â‚…NO]آ²âپ؛ (nitrosyl iron), in which iron is in the +1 oxidation state. Option (1).

text: The pair of transition metal ions that are both colourless in aqueous solution is:
explanation: Colour in transition-metal ions arises from dâ€“d transitions, which require partially filled d orbitals. Scآ³âپ؛ is dâپ° and Znآ²âپ؛ is dآ¹âپ°; both lack possible dâ€“d transitions and are colourless. The other ions have partly filled d orbitals and are coloured. Option (2).

text: In the metallurgy of iron in a blast furnace, the role of limestone (CaCOâ‚ƒ) is to:
options: Act as a reducing agent for iron oxide
Form slag by combining with silica impurities
Lower the melting point of iron only
explanation: Limestone decomposes to CaO, which combines with the acidic SiOâ‚‚ gangue to form fusible calcium silicate slag (CaSiOâ‚ƒ). This removes the silica impurity. Reduction is done by CO/coke. Option (2).

text: Which of the following is the correct order of basic strength in aqueous solution for group 1 and 2 hydroxides?
explanation: Down a group the metalâ€“OH bond becomes more ionic and the hydroxide more basic. For alkali metals: LiOH < NaOH < KOH < RbOH < CsOH. Option (4).

text: Hydrogen peroxide (Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚) acts as both an oxidising and a reducing agent. When it reduces acidified KMnOâ‚„, the oxidation state of oxygen in Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚ changes from:
explanation: In Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚ oxygen is in the âˆ’1 state. As a reducing agent toward KMnOâ‚„ it is oxidised to Oâ‚‚ (oxidation state 0), releasing electrons that reduce Mn. So O goes from âˆ’1 to 0. Option (1).

text: The most stable carbocation among the following is:
explanation: Carbocation stability increases with the number of electron-donating alkyl groups (+I and hyperconjugation): 3آ° > 2آ° > 1آ° > methyl. The tert-butyl cation is the most stable. Option (4).

text: The number of structural (chain + position) isomers possible for the molecular formula Câ‚…Hâ‚پâ‚‚ is:
explanation: Câ‚…Hâ‚پâ‚‚ (pentane) has three structural isomers: n-pentane, 2-methylbutane (isopentane) and 2,2-dimethylpropane (neopentane). Option (2).

text: Which of the following compounds will show geometrical (cisâ€“trans) isomerism?
explanation: Geometrical isomerism requires each doubly-bonded carbon to bear two different groups. In 2-butene each spآ² carbon has H and CHâ‚ƒ (different), giving cis and trans forms. The others have a =CHâ‚‚ or two identical groups on one carbon, so no cis/trans. Option (1).

text: Markovnikov's rule is best illustrated by the addition of HBr to propene, which gives predominantly:
explanation: By Markovnikov's rule, H adds to the carbon bearing more H atoms; Br adds to the more substituted carbon (forming the more stable 2آ° carbocation). Propene + HBr â†’ 2-bromopropane (CHâ‚ƒCHBrCHâ‚ƒ). Option (2).

text: In the reaction of CHâ‚ƒBr with aqueous KOH, the mechanism predominantly followed is:
options: SN2 with backside attack and inversion
explanation: Methyl (and primary) halides undergo SN2: the nucleophile OHâپ» attacks the carbon from the side opposite to the leaving group in a single concerted step, giving inversion of configuration. SN1 is favoured for 3آ° halides. Option (2).

text: Chlorobenzene is much less reactive than chloromethane toward nucleophilic substitution mainly because:
options: Chlorine in chlorobenzene is more electronegative
The Câ€“Cl bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance
explanation: In chlorobenzene the lone pair on chlorine delocalises into the ring, giving the Câ€“Cl bond partial double-bond character. This shortens and strengthens the bond, making the Câ€“Cl harder to break, so nucleophilic substitution is very difficult. Option (2).

text: Phenol is more acidic than ethanol primarily because:
options: Phenol has a higher molecular mass
Phenol contains a benzene ring that is basic
explanation: On losing Hâپ؛, phenol forms the phenoxide ion whose negative charge is delocalised over the aromatic ring by resonance, stabilising it. The ethoxide ion from ethanol has no such stabilisation, so phenol (pKa â‰ˆ 10) is far more acidic than ethanol (pKa â‰ˆ 16). Option (2).

text: Williamson's synthesis is the best method for preparing:
options: Both symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers from alkyl halide and alkoxide
explanation: Williamson's synthesis reacts a sodium alkoxide (Râ€“ Oâپ»Naâپ؛) with an alkyl halide (R'â€“X) via SN2 to give Râ€“Oâ€“R'. It works for both symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers; primary alkyl halides give the best yields. Option (2).

text: Which of the following will give a positive iodoform test?
explanation: The iodoform test is positive for compounds containing a CHâ‚ƒCOâ€“ group or a CHâ‚ƒCH(OH)â€“ group (which can be oxidised to CHâ‚ƒCOâ€“). Ethanol has the CHâ‚ƒCH(OH)â€“ unit and gives a positive test. Methanol, propan-1-ol and ethers lack this group. Option (1).

text: The correct order of acidic strength among the following carboxylic acids is:
explanation: Electron-withdrawing Cl atoms (âˆ’I effect) stabilise the carboxylate anion, increasing acidity. More Cl atoms = stronger acid. Hence trichloroacetic > dichloroacetic > monochloroacetic > acetic acid. Option (2).

text: Aniline (Câ‚†Hâ‚…NHâ‚‚) is a weaker base than ethylamine because:
options: Aniline has a smaller molecular mass
The lone pair on N is delocalised into the benzene ring
explanation: In aniline the nitrogen lone pair is delocalised into the aromatic ring by resonance, making it less available for protonation. In ethylamine the lone pair is fully available and the alkyl group is electron-donating (+I), so ethylamine is a stronger base. Option (2).

text: When aniline is treated with NaNOâ‚‚ and dilute HCl at 0â€“5 آ°C, the product formed is:
explanation: Primary aromatic amines undergo diazotisation with nitrous acid (NaNOâ‚‚ + HCl) at low temperature (0â€“5 آ°C) to give benzene diazonium chloride (Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ»), which is stable only when cold. Option (2).

text: The bond that links two monosaccharide units in a disaccharide such as sucrose is called:
explanation: Monosaccharide units are joined by a glycosidic linkage formed between the anomeric carbon of one sugar and a hydroxyl of another with loss of water. In sucrose it is an خ±,خ²-1,2-glycosidic bond. Option (2).

text: Which of the following statements about proteins is correct?
options: The secondary structure (خ±-helix/خ²-sheet) is stabilised by hydrogen bonds
Peptide bonds are formed between two hydroxyl groups
All proteins are made of D-amino acids
explanation: Proteins are polymers of خ±-amino acids (naturally L-form) linked by peptide bonds (between â€“COOH and â€“NHâ‚‚). The secondary structure (خ±-helix and خ²-pleated sheet) is stabilised by hydrogen bonding between the C=O and Nâ€“H groups of the backbone. Option (2).

text: Which of the following is an example of a condensation (step-growth) polymer?
explanation: Nylon-6,6 is formed by condensation polymerisation between hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid with elimination of water. Polythene, PVC and polypropylene are addition (chain-growth) polymers of alkene monomers. Option (2).

text: Aspirin, a well-known drug, acts pharmacologically as an:
explanation: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) relieves pain (analgesic) and reduces fever (antipyretic); it also has anti-inflammatory and anti-blood-clotting effects. It is not an antibiotic, antacid or antihistamine. Option (3).

text: Sodium benzoate is commonly added to food and beverages to act as a:
explanation: Sodium benzoate is a widely used food preservative; it inhibits the growth of microorganisms (especially in acidic foods), preventing spoilage. Sweeteners (e.g., saccharin), antioxidants (e.g., BHT) and dyes serve other purposes. Option (2).
